data.title: Carnival: Drifting Along With Shareholders' Returns
data.description:
Carnival Corporation & plc (NYSE:CCL) (NYSE:CUK), once a thriving business providing leisure travel services to millions of people, is now a shadow of its former self. Its leading position in the industry was unquestionable. Carnival Corporation grew its revenues and profits, maintaining high margins and stable cash flows, while also rewarding its shareholders with consistent dividend payments. During its time of dominance, the company continued to expand through strategic acquisitions, which gave it the status of an empire among cruise lines.

This idyllic environment continued until January 22, 2018, when the stock reached its all-time high at $71.65. This was the peak. The price deteriorated along with the sentiment surrounding the company. Competition continued to intensify, and a $20.0 million pollution fine was one of the factors hurting the brand. Besides that, investors started turning towards high-growth tech names, abandoning Carnival Corporation just like passengers abandon a sinking ship.

The collapse came in February and March 2020, when the pandemic crippled the world. The crisis exposed the business, its dependencies, defenselessness, and obsolescence to the global economy. Five years later, it can be said that the company has recovered. On the surface, it seems so, but a deeper look gives a different perspective on the cruise line's overall financial condition.

Is Carnival Corporation A Great Business?

When analyzing a business, that's the first question a value investor should ask before even looking at the company's financials and metrics. In the case of Carnival Corporation, not only is the company not a great business—it's a terrible one.

The cruise line provides travel services in the leisure segment for individuals. The company's offering is obsolete from an economic standpoint. At this point, the analysis should end, and an investor should move forward to the next investment idea. Does it mean that money can't be made by investing in Carnival Corporation? Of course not. Money can be made, and the stock may deliver spectacular gains in the future.

Yet, the idea of value investing is to acquire a great business at a discount and reap the rewards in the long run. It means having peace of mind while real earnings grow, dividends are paid, and shares are repurchased, increasing an investor's ownership stake in the business. However, such a scenario hasn't and likely won't materialize for the shareholders of Carnival Corporation.

From a customer perspective, a personal budget for leisure activities is the first to be cut when financial hardships occur. A cruise trip is a nice-to-have experience and something one can easily give up on. When a crisis hits a household, Carnival Corporation's services become irrelevant.

Scaling it from a household to a global level, the pandemic proved the unimportance of the business to the global economy. Carnival Corporation was classified as a non-essential business and was at the bottom of the government's list in terms of financial relief, reopening rules, and operational allowance.

As if that weren't enough, the company was exempt from financial assistance due to its domicile. Carnival Corporation is not a U.S. company in a legal sense, as it's incorporated in Panama. During the pandemic, only American companies received financial aid, and the cruise line was left out. A theoretically smart move to register the business abroad to avoid paying income tax in the homeland turned into a nail in the coffin during the global emergency in 2020.

The Carnival Corporation's non-essential character, combined with the pain brought to its customers and shareholders in one of the worst crises in decades due to its foreign domicile, should be enough to stay away from the business. The company has clearly no moat. If it did, its revenue wouldn't have dropped 90.1% between 2020 and 2022 while numerous companies were thriving.

Carnival Corporation Dilutes Its Shareholders

The stock price has been trending upward, gaining 97.88% over the last year. Factor grades flash green, as well as recommendations on Seeking Alpha, among Wall Street analysts, and finally, a Strong Buy rating from the Quant. Yet, the shareholders are silently ripped off through dilution of their stake in the business, along with decreasing profits tied to their piece of ownership.

A quick look at Carnival Corporation's financials provides insight into how these practices affect investors.

2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
Revenue (billion) $15.7 $16.4 $17.5 $18.9 $20.8 $5.6 $1.9 $12.2 $21.6 $25.0
Revenue p. share $20.2 $22.0 $24.3 $26.6 $30.2 $7.2 $1.7 $10.3 $17.1 $19.6

Revenues and revenues per share over the last 10 years.

A shareholder who has kept his shares for the last ten years saw a total increase in revenues of 59.2%. However, what he has experienced as an owner of the business is a sales decline of -2.9%. It doesn't get better when analyzing the bottom line. A combined net profit growth from FY 2015 to FY 2024 amounts to 9.0%. Earnings per share over the same period of time declined by a staggering 33.6%. A Carnival Corporation investor's profits from the business are $1.50 a share, compared to $2.26 in 2015, which is frustrating. Unfortunately, a dilution works against shareholders.

The company used to buy back its shares for extended periods of time. However, the pandemic's distress forced management to take the opposite direction to save the business from bankruptcy. It feels like the health crisis is long gone, but the release of shares continues. Only the pace slowed down.

Carnival Corporation's Financial Health

Growth estimates are promising, particularly with regard to free cash flow, which is expected to grow by 59.09%. While the projection seems exciting at first glance, it's worth analyzing the composition of the metric.

The company's free cash flow is calculated as Capital Expenditures subtracted from Cash from Operations. One of the components of Cash from Operations is Depreciation & Amortization. While Depreciation & Amortization itself is a non-cash charge, it represents the ongoing economic cost of maintaining and replacing productive assets. Excluding the add-back ensures that free cash flow more accurately reflects the long-term cash requirements of the business, rather than just its short-term accounting profit.

2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024

FCF (billion)

 $2.3 $2.1 $2.4 $1.8 $0.0 -$9.9 -$7.7 -$6.6 $1.0 $1.3
FCF - Dep&Am (billion) $0.6 $0.3 $0.5 -$0.2 -$.2.1 -$12.1 $-9.9 -$8.9 -$1.4 -$1.3

FCF and FCF less Depreciation & Amortization over the last 10 years.

Considering Depreciation & Amortization as actual recurring expenses, Free Cash Flow doesn't look so encouraging anymore. The burden of these costs can also be seen when examining what percentage of Gross Profit, Depreciation & Amortization constitutes. Over the last decade, it has ranged from a low of 19% in 2024 to a high of 269% in 2020. Overall, Depreciation & Amortization was consistently above 20%. In comparison, Nike (NKE), a business with a strong competitive advantage, achieved a ratio of 4.0% in its most recent fiscal year.

A metric which individual investors often overlook is Retained Earnings. Unfortunately, it's another one that doesn't lift the spirits of the shareholders.

Retained Earnings are the portion of a company’s profits that are kept in the business instead of being paid out as dividends. Over time, they accumulate to show how much profit the company has reinvested in itself since its founding. The graph demonstrates the devastation that happened between 2020 and 2023. Fixed costs and debt service, combined with no revenue generated, slaughtered Retained Earnings. The company's "net worth" collapsed by 92%.

Carnival Corporation's Opportunities

On the 29th of September, Carnival Corporation will announce earnings for the third quarter of FY2025. The market is expecting EPS GAAP of $1.31, which would be a 3.56% increase YoY. Further consensus for the upcoming quarters is also promising, suggesting strong ongoing momentum.

The sentiment surrounding the company is optimistic. The management proudly emphasizes the business's achievements and sets the bar high for the future. As the CEO, Joshua Ian Weinstein, put it during the last conference call:

This marks 8 quarters in a row we've achieved record revenues on record yields. We also hit new second quarter highs for EBITDA and operating income, both in total and on a per ALBD basis, while customer deposits also reached an all-time high.

Indeed, the company flourishes despite the dismal pandemic and its aftermath, which now seems like a distant past. The operating margin has returned to pre-pandemic levels, and guidance has been raised for the second time this fiscal year, although it concerns adjusted metrics such as cruise costs excl. fuel, EBITDA, Net Income, and EPS.

Another positive sign of the business revival is the investment in the highest-returning brands, including AIDA Cruises, expected in FY 2030 and 2032, respectively. Additionally, Carnival Cruise Line officially opened Celebration Key in July, its new private destination on Grand Bahama. Carnival Vista was the first ship to dock, bringing nearly 5,000 guests to enjoy the island’s charm. Visitors could enjoy the world’s tallest sandcastle and the Caribbean’s biggest freshwater lagoons, among others.

The ships are getting upgrades, with AIDAdiva recently completing the AIDA Evolution program, which features multiple enhancements, being the first of seven ships. In the near future, Carnival Cruise Line will introduce Carnival Festivale and Carnival Tropicale in 2027 and 2028. Additionally, Star Princess will join the award-winning Sun Princess in Q4 FY 2025.

These developments are undoubtedly uplifting for anybody cheering Carnival Corporation. Reinvestments in the business will likely remain the main growth driver, attracting customers and enabling the business to expand. Revenue and profits should follow.

Carnival Corporation's Valuation

The company might be considered undervalued due to its low P/E ratio. While it's on the lower end compared with the post-pandemic years, the multiple hasn't been higher since the middle of 2018.

Also, the P/FCF ratio reaching 29.72 doesn't suggest that the business is trading below its fair value.

To look closer at the company's valuation, a Discounted Earnings Model was created with the following assumptions:

  • Earnings will grow at a rate of.
  • Growth is expected to continue at a lower rate of 10.8% CAGR over the subsequent 5 years.
  • Perpetual growth will be at a conservative 2.0% annually.
  • Discount rate amounts to 10.0%.
  • Margin of safety is set to 20.0%.

The calculation suggests that the company is undervalued by a staggering 41.2%. It may be true as long as Carnival Corporation posts earnings growth as specified above. An investor should ask himself whether such a bet is worth the hassle and the uncertainty associated with investing in such a business. Most likely, these EPS growth projections don't account for dilution, and that's a powerful weapon against shareholders.

Will Carnival Drift or Sail Ahead?

Currently, there is excitement surrounding the business. Such a positive sentiment, combined with optimistic messages from management, encourages investors to invest in Carnival Corporation. This may temporarily boost the stock price. Besides that, a relatively low valuation may attract buyers.

At the same time, the company is a terrible business with poor financials and a long track record of struggle on multiple fronts. The culmination came during the most recent global health crisis, which proved that the company is, to put it mildly, non-essential to the economy. The dilution of shareholders is another red flag that should put anybody interested in the company on alert.

It's impossible to predict where the stock price will go in the short term. Therefore, money can be made regardless of the quality of the business when bought and sold at the right time. However, Carnival Corporation is not a bet a value investor would like to make and sail ahead into the open sea.

data.title: Via: Proof Of Profits Will Be Key For This IPO To Take Off
data.description:
Amid red-hot equity markets this year, the IPO window is suddenly wide open, with many high-profile private companies rushing to take advantage of market euphoria to make their public debuts.

Among them is Via Transportation (NYSE:VIA), which was a one-time competitor to Uber (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT) in the United States. Since then, Via has switched to becoming a software vendor, helping to create operational software solutions for mass public transit networks in the United States and Europe.

The IPO priced at $46, and in what is an atypical twist for new issuances, Via has largely failed to generate post-IPO momentum since going public on Sept. 12.

I am quite skeptical of the stock's prospects. While Via isn't ridiculously overvalued, the company has a stark margin contrast against most SaaS software peers, which makes it far more likely that investors will look more to the company's currently negative bottom line results than take a leap of faith on Via's revenue growth. I'm initiating Via at a "Sell" rating.

Via: The software platform for public transit

First, let's kick off by getting a solid understanding of what Via does. When Via was founded in 2012, it aimed to compete with the then-nascent Uber (UBER) and Lyft (LYFT), particularly in its home market in New York City. It saw itself as the rideshare of choice for the daily city commute.

Over time, however, Via recognized the basic truth of the rideshare marketplace: It requires economies of scale. Recognizing that it was destined to trail behind the two major players, Via switched to becoming a SaaS software platform. It is what we consider a "vertical" software platform that specializes in addressing the problems of a single industry. In this case, public transportation.

The chart above showcases the five core pillars of Via's software. Its planning and scheduling tools are the heart of the product. Leveraging a transportation networks' own data, Via can predict ridership trends, which allows a municipal authority to better plan transit schedules and routes. Once schedules are locked in, the software also incorporates a labor scheduling and dispatch tool, which assigns drivers to specific routes and incorporates their own work hours, available vehicles, and local labor rules.

Via's software also comprises customer/rider-facing tools. Via creates white-labeled apps (as in, branded by the local public transport network) for riders to plan for and pay for rides.

Via estimates that the global public transportation industry is valued at $545 billion, with its core markets in North America and Europe representing $250 billion of that market opportunity. Meanwhile, Via estimates its serviceable market opportunity ("SAM"), which is the revenue potential from this total market, at $82 billion in North America and Europe alone. Needless to say, Via's current annualized revenue run rate (at ~$0.4 billion) represents a small fraction of what its total market opportunity is.

Financial overview: Watch the margin gap to other software companies

Now, let's turn the discussion to Via's financials. The latest reported results are showcased in the snapshot below:

Via's revenue grew 27% y/y to $205.8 million in the first half of FY 2025. Its annualized platform revenue run rate, meanwhile, grew at a faster 34% y/y clip to $429 million. It's important to note the positives here: Via noted that its customer base has over a 120% net revenue retention rate, indicating that the average customer expands their spending on the platform by 20% on a y/y basis. This is a powerful engine for growth in a recurring-revenue software company.

However, what is slightly concerning to me is the nature of Via's business. By its own admission, the public transportation industry is highly fragmented and siloed, and while many public transportation networks could stand to benefit from greater software penetration, there is no one-size-fits-all solution for public transportation. This is an entirely different beast from, say, Salesforce (CRM) – where the same Sales Cloud product can run decently well for different types of salespeople.

As an example of the kinds of customization needed, Via's scheduling tools require compliance with local labor laws. In perhaps the biggest need for customization yet, each customer-facing application is also white labeled. This means that every sale requires upfront customization and professional services, a reality that is reflected in Via's gross margin at just 40% in the first half of the year.

Via's gross margin profile is about half of most of its SaaS peers, which typically see pro forma gross margin profiles in the 70%-80% range. Most SaaS companies have a highly scalable business model. They invest heavily in R&D upfront to create a one-size-fits-all product, and then the high gross margin profile allows them to enjoy large economies of scale as they land more customers. In Via's case, however, it is more of a cross between a bona fide software platform and a consulting or tech integration firm with lower services margins.

Via's profitability, while improving, is still negative. Adjusted EBITDA margins in the first half of FY 2025 were only -8%, up 11 points y/y.

This doesn't mean Via's entire business model is unsalvageable. The key lies in customer retention and expansion. The company, after all, cited 20%+ net expansion rates. As long as the company can drive expansion within the customer base, it can achieve revenue growth after deploying significant upfront resources in sales and product customization. It will just take longer to reach a mature stage than other software companies.

Valuation and key takeaways

Post-IPO, Via is now sitting at a ~$48 price. There are 79.357 million shares post-IPO, giving Via a market cap of $3.81 billion.

Meanwhile, if we net off the $78.2 million of cash plus $301.9 million of expected IPO proceeds against $57.6 million of drawn debt on Via's latest June balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $3.49 billion.

Over the trailing 12 months, Via has generated $375.8 million of revenue. If we extrapolate the company's current 27% y/y growth rate forward, the company would generate $477.2 million in revenue. This positions Via's valuation at 7.3x EV/forward revenue.

Now, when we compare Via against other ~20% growth software stocks, a ~7x forward multiple doesn't seem too rich, considering peers are at a ~7x-10x range. Yet we can't forget the fact that Via's revenue stream is half as valuable, thanks to the significant gross margin gap to most software peers.

In my view, Via will need to prove itself with true adjusted EBITDA/bottom line expansion before the stock has an ability to take off. I'm a skeptic on this IPO; remain on the sidelines for now. In my view, the stock will be available to purchase below its IPO price eventually.

data.title: Amazon: Buy The Worst-Performing Mag 7 Stock
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) belongs to the infamous Mag 7 that have fallen in and out of favor this year with the ups and downs of tariffs, macro uncertainty with the Fed holding rates up until this month, and increasing competition on the cloud compute front. The result has been a lagging stock price performance. Amazon is the last to escape the red on the YTD chart, currently trading flat, below all Mag 7 and the S&P 500 (SP500) benchmark, and below its estimated fair value of $266.56 by +17%. This is why it’s on our radar as best positioned for upside.

RSI is very attractive at 39, nearing oversold territory. The stock’s next support is EMA200 at $212. Amazon stock is pretty much in free fall; it broke the EMA21 and the EMA50 with high volume on sell-off and is heading to test the EMA200.

Valuation isn’t too hot either. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 33.19x against a sector average of 18.02x and a forward EV/Sales of 3.42x versus a sector average of 1.35x. However, if you peg Amazon’s EV/Sales against the peer group, it’s clear that Amazon is most discounted at current levels.

The market hasn’t been expecting much from Amazon this year, but that’s starting to change after Wells Fargo boosted its rating to 'overweight' this morning, based on greater conviction in AWS revenue acceleration next year, driven by Project Rainer capacity, a dedicated AWS data center specifically designed for Anthropic compute. The company also gained about 51 upward revisions versus only one downward revision over the past three months, indicating a potentially better Q4 report. Remember that this quarter, Amazon stock took a nosedive post-earnings, dropping as much as 8%. This, combined with the stock price performance QTD (which is flat), makes us more bullish on Amazon next year, as the worst has been priced in this year. We see an attractive, longer-term buy opportunity for Amazon at current levels.

The stock got weighed down by two big themes that are now looking up

1. Tariff headaches were a big point of fear, considering its North America e-commerce business makes up the bulk of its sales at $100.1B in Q2, up 11% Y/Y, and relies heavily not only on imports from China but also on de minimis exemption (which strikes off tariffs for goods under $800). The market has priced in this headwind, in our opinion, and the temporary pause on U.S.-China tariffs has provided a big enough window to stock up strategically on inventory for retail business. The next deadline for a trade update is mid-November, and a lot hangs in the balance on what the two nations decide. Given the work done to secure a TikTok deal, we’re more confident that the tone has shifted from aggressive to friendly. So we see limited downside on this front.

Aside from better U.S.-China relations, the other factor that’ll work in Amazon’s favor is lower interest rates. We got the first cut of the year this month with Powell cutting a quarter point. Now, two more are expected by the end of 2025. We believe this will present a more favorable environment for Amazon, both in terms of logistics and infrastructure investment, as well as consumer spending power. And let’s not forget that Thanksgiving and Christmas are coming up, and historically speaking, this is when Amazon shines, with the added bonus of the two largest consumer spending events. There’s a seasonal boost in the third and fourth quarters of the year, specifically in the latter, as shown below.

2. The other black dot on Amazon was a slower growth rate of AWS compared to Microsoft (MSFT) Azure or Alphabet (GOOGL) Google Cloud, in spite of holding the largest market share within cloud computing. This became a growing point of concern this quarter, with Azure and Google Cloud's growth rates outpacing AWS at 39% and 32%, respectively, compared to 18%. Operating income for the segment also came in lower than expected at $10.2B versus $10.9B. The good news is that the market has digested this negative. There is a threat in terms of share loss to Azure, specifically; however, we think with proper execution on new projects, AWS will retain its position as the largest share with 30%.

There are also catalysts working in AWS's favor that prompted Wells Fargo to raise 2026 AWS growth guidance from 19% to 22%, which "estimated that the facility supports ~$14B annual AWS revenue at full capacity of 2.2GW." This was the logic of their upgrade:

We see Project Rainier, a significant compute capacity build w/ partner Anthropic, as the primary driver of acceleration, contributing 5%/4% to AWS growth in ‘26/’27.

There should be room for growth, with management noting on the Q2 call that demand outpaces capacity. Amazon, in addition to Google, is at the forefront of the AI custom ASIC race. The Trainium 2 is the "backbone" for Claude models and Bedrock, with 30-40% better pricing than GPU alternatives. We're expecting to see more updates on the custom ASIC front in 2026. At the end of the day, Amazon is outspending the rest of the tier 1 players on CapEx for the year, committing $100B, as shown below.

Chips on its shoulder

Things aren't all smooth sailing for Amazon. Earlier this week, the company announced plans to close down all of its Amazon Fresh stores based in the UK on the grounds that “the concept of contactless shopping failed to gain traction once pandemic restrictions were lifted.” The good news is that this signals efficiency; we’re not seeing management force a failed project. Instead, we’re seeing them adapt to a changing environment with plans to convert some to Whole Foods. To us, the takeaway is that Amazon is testing what the average consumer is ready to accept as the company pushes harder into the grocery delivery segment. The target is to double the Prime members in the UK who have access to three or more online grocery choices. The online grocery market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 26.83% between 2025 and 2025. We think Amazon will continue to use its optimization strategy to boost its positioning on this front and gain share.

The other curveball on Amazon’s dashboard is President Trump’s new H-1B visa fee of $100,000, which disproportionately will weigh on Amazon, which has had the most H-1B approvals annually under its belt since 2020. This year alone, Amazon has had 12,391 H-1B visa beneficiaries and will be looking at a hefty fee for any added visas next year. This could be a big concern down the line, but not one we see weighing on Amazon’s finances in the near term, considering that current visa holders are protected.

Bottom line

We’ve seen all the Mag 7 players get pushed down around, and all but Amazon have recovered.

It’s unlikely, in our opinion, that Amazon stock will end the year flat or even back in the red with the S&P 500 trading at record highs, as we see green shoots in the business. We’re doubling down on our buy rating on Amazon and see this as an attractive window to add.

data.title: Manhattan Associates: The Valuation Expansion Has Gone Too Far
data.description:
Since my last coverage of Manhattan Associates, Inc. (NASDAQ:MANH), where I upgraded its rating from hold to buy amid the tariff panic, the stock has performed above expectations (+26.7% return), and the management has recently raised its full-year guidance.

However, the valuation multiple has substantially expanded as a result of stronger-than-anticipated demand for Manhattan's cloud platform and improved profitability.

In this article, I will review the most recent developments seeking to determine whether the stock is fairly priced or it is time to take some chips off the table.

Company Overview

Manhattan Associates started developing warehouse management systems (WMS) in the early 90s. Since then, it has expanded its product offering to everything related to supply chains and logistics across many different verticals, such as retail, transport, and manufacturing, among others.

In 2017, like many other software companies, MANH transitioned to a cloud-native model, creating a predictable cash flow stream through a recurring revenue model.

While the industry is highly fragmented and MANH faces competition from other providers such as Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), SAP (NYSE:SAP), and Blue Yonder, the product is one of the stickiest I am aware of.

In addition to the burden of switching such a mission-critical software to another provider, with the inherent risk of disrupting operations, MANH sells its products under multi-year agreements of five or more years.

Despite competition from larger companies, MANH's focus on developing a top-notch product has yielded outstanding returns, delivering an over 20% CAGR over the last two decades, reaching a $13.2B market cap.

Margin Expansion Despite Incremental Spending

Although MANH's profitability suffered a temporary decline during the transition to the cloud, margins have been recovering in recent years, and the most recent quarter has been no exception.

In Q2 2025, MANH delivered a 27.1% operating margin, which represents a 140 bps expansion compared to the previous year. But, in my view, the most remarkable achievement is not the impressive margin expansion, but the fact that it has been accomplished while investing in future growth.

While some high-quality businesses can squeeze as many dollars as possible during a period of time by raising prices and reducing costs, MANH is not in a position to do so due to the competitive landscape. Instead, MANH needs to constantly develop its products.

At the moment, the management is focused on two fronts: reaching more customers and integrating AI into its platform.

Starting this fall, each Manhattan Active platform application will include purpose-built agents for each app's respective roles and personas.

These agents will intelligently automate and optimize processes, allowing associates to be faster and far more effective.

Source: Eric A. Clark (CEO) - Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

While expenses on personnel dedicated to IT and R&D are under control, SG&A expenses increased by 23% y/y due to significant investments in sales and marketing. The company just increased its sales team, appointed a new Chief Sales Officer, and expanded its partnership with Shopify Inc. (NASDAQ:SHOP).

Cloud Growth Accelerates

The most positive news during Q2 has been, in my view, the increase in growth rates in the cloud platform and the record $2B of remaining performance obligations (RPO).

As more customers migrate from on-premise software to the Manhattan Active Platform, growth rates naturally decline, but the most recent quarter came in above expectations.

Moreover, the management reaffirmed its confidence in delivering above 20% y/y growth rates in the foreseeable future:

Our bookings and pipeline continue to be solid. Our TAM is expanding. As I mentioned, we're making changes to accelerate sales velocity.

That includes specialization, and that specialization around products, around renewals, around conversions, strengthening partnerships, adding sales talent.

Source: Eric A. Clark (CEO) - Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

Taking into account that RPO grew even faster than cloud revenues, showing a 26% y/y increase, I believe MANH won't disappoint in the upcoming quarters.

Services Moderate Its Decline

Unlike the cloud segment, which generates recurring revenues, the services segment is more cyclical, since customers are billed hourly when they need training, to implement new solutions, or customizations.

As a result, customers can delay some spending if the macro situation gets ugly or the confidence in the economy worsens. As shown in the chart below, since late 2024, the consumer sentiment has declined from 72 to 61.7 points.

After two years of improving consumer sentiment, the situation started to deteriorate following the Trump election and the tariff chaos, resulting in two consecutive quarters of declining services revenues.

However, the 6% decline in service revenues during the most recent quarter was above expectations, and it improved sequentially from the 8% decline in Q1.

While the management remains cautious about this part of the business, I believe that the recent rate cut and the improved visibility about the impact of tariffs will have a positive impact in the upcoming quarters.

That's the part of our business that customers have a lot of flexibility. It's time and material contracts. And if they want to put their foot on the throttle and really go fast, we can help them do that.

But if they want to slow down, we do that with them as well. So we're just taking a conservative approach.

Source: Eric A. Clark (CEO) - Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

Guidance and Expected Growth

As a result of the strong demand from new clients (70% of the revenue increase is from new users), the management has raised its full-year guidance.

Some readers might wonder why EPS is expected to decline even with revenue growth and margin expansion. The reason is that the tax rate will increase to 21% this year, compared to 18.2% in 2024, when the company benefited from higher R&D and SBC tax benefits.

From a long-term perspective, the industry is expected to grow between 8% and 11.3% over the next years, depending on the research consulted, which is in line with the consensus estimates of 11% CAGR in MANH's revenues until 2034.

Given that the cloud segment is growing faster than services and its contribution to overall revenues increases, margins will keep expanding. In contrast with the services segment, where MANH needs to hire more consultants to scale, the cloud segment has much higher operating leverage.

Furthermore, many early cloud clients will be renewing their contracts in 2026 and 2027, which will allow MANH to rise prices and execute cross-selling opportunities.

So when those customers that are going to be renewing in '26, when they bought Warehouse Management, we didn't have Transportation Management or Supply Chain Management or AI agents.

Source: Eric A. Clark (CEO) - Q2 2025 Earnings Call.

Valuation Expansion

Although MANH's fundamentals have slightly improved in the most recent quarter, the valuation has significantly expanded since my last coverage in late April, as shown in the chart below.

In my view, the stock was trading around fair value back then, and the 5% increase in EPS expectations doesn't justify a 20% valuation multiple expansion.

By discounting future cash flows at a 9% discount rate, MANH's stock would be fairly priced at $155 per share, and the expected returns from the current stock price are below the long-term market returns.

In the model presented above, I expect revenues to grow around 10% per year, margins to keep expanding as MANH benefits from operating leverage and the higher contribution from the cloud segment, and a 1.5% yearly decline in the share count as a result of buybacks.

Risks

MANH has a solid balance sheet with zero long-term debt and operates in a fast-growing industry, but there are some risks worth considering.

First, I would highlight the retirement of one of the co-founders and the recent step down of Eddie Capel, who served as CEO for over a decade and led the development of MANH's cloud platform.

Although the transition is evolving smoothly and he is still involved as Vice Chairman of the Board, it always gives me more confidence to see founders and executives with a proven track record of scaling the company.

The other major risk, as always in companies operating in fragmented industries with products that need to be updated to remain competitive, is the constant need to reinvest in R&D.

In relation to that, I have noticed that customer reviews have worsened since my first coverage of the company in late 2023. Back then, Manhattan's Supply Chain platform had an 8 out of 10 rating in TrustRadius, but it has now declined, as shown in the image below.

While the sample is small and it might not be representative, since every new review has a substantial impact, this is something I will keep monitoring.

Conclusion

The sentiment improvement around MANH's stock is well supported by the most recent results and the increased guidance. Moreover, some uncertainty around tariffs has faded, and the company has room to grow its revenues at double-digit rates in the years to come.

However, the over 20% expansion in the valuation multiple in recent months is, in my view, excessive, and the stock is trading at a premium to its fair value of $155 per share.

Therefore, I am downgrading MANH's stock from buy to hold until its price approaches the estimated fair value.

data.title: Alibaba: Time For A Higher Valuation
data.description:
An article I wrote on China's Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) a year and a half ago was titled "Alibaba: The AI Bet Has Potential". That potential is now paying off, as is evident in the stock's 36% price rise since I last wrote an update in May 2025. This far exceeds the 22% upside for 2025 I had forecast at that time.

Despite the bigger than expected gains, here I make a case for an even higher valuation for BABA. Its recent AI related developments and their impact is notable and can driven BABA forward beyond what traditional market valuations would imply.

The AI effect on BABA

In the company's "Letter from our Chairman and our CEO" released in June this year, Alibaba underlined its focus on AI, which first started becoming clear with Eddie Wu's promotion to the top job in 2023. It mentioned that "AI will be the primary driver of incremental value and transformation", with a three part approach to supporting this including investments in AI and cloud infrastructure, developing AI models further and using AI for its existing businesses.

The promise of AI has also enthused investors as evident in BABA's price patterns in recent months. Three AI related developments for the company are correlated with price spikes for BABA. Here are the details:

  • Qwen continues to make waves

On September 5, the company shared a look at its latest model Qwen3-Max-Preview, saying that it outperforms a former version. Within a week of this news, the stock was up by 12.4%. This should come as no surprise considering the general investor bullishness on AI and how investors have responded to earlier developments related to Qwen.

The company first shared that its large language model team had developed the AI model as well (see section on 'AI developments' in the link) in January. Specifically, it was said to be more competitive than the highly cost effective DeepSeek's (DEEPSEEK) model V3. BABA rose by 7% in a single trading session following this development. And then by another ~9% after it released the reasoning model QwQ-32B in early March.

  • Fund raising for AI investments

The stock rose by another 8% on September 11 after the company issued notes to fund investments, particularly in AI. In its press release, Alibaba said that it:

...will use the net proceeds...with a strategic focus on strengthening its cloud infrastructure capabilities and international commerce business operations. Specifically, the allocation will include approximately 80% directed towards enhancing its cloud infrastructure, which encompasses scaling up data centers, upgrading technology, and improving services to meet growing demand.....".

  • Triple-digit AI product revenue growth

Unlike the other two developments, this one is only partially AI driven, but the segment is likely to have had a sentimental impact here too. This was on August 29, following the company's Q1 FY26 (quarter ending June 2025) results, when BABA saw a significant 13% uptick in a single trading session.

With a 26% YoY increase, the company's Cloud Intelligence Group saw the fastest revenue growth during the quarter. Specifically with regards to AI, the company points out "AI-related product revenue maintained triple-digit year-over-year growth for the eighth consecutive quarter.".

Even though the Cloud Intelligence segment's contribution to revenues is still small at 13.5% as of Q1 FY26, it's inching up considering that the division brought in 10.9% of the revenues in Q1 FY25. Also, its contribution to adjusted EBITA has risen to 7.6% compared to 5.2% in Q1 FY25.

While the increased contribution of the segment's adjusted EBITA is related to the 26% YoY increase in the number, it's also because of a contraction in the overall adjusted EBITA for Alibaba. In fact, this is another distinguishing factor for the segment's profits. It's one of the two segments to bring in profits, the other being the domestic e-commerce business and the only segment to see an expansion in profits.

Estimating a fair market valuation

With the promise shown by AI in particular and cloud intelligence in general, the question now is - what should BABA's fair valuation be? After all, AI stocks are trading at a premium.

On the other hand, going purely by its financials, and not its potential, Alibaba is still largely an e-commerce stock, with historically lower valuations. The domestic and international e-commerce businesses put together still bring in over 70% of the company's revenues and most of its adjusted EBITA comes from domestic e-commerce.

To estimate the fair valuation for the stock, both sides of the story are considered here.

  • The AI related valuation

To estimate BABA's potential valuation based on its AI prospects, the example of the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF (AIQ) has been considered. The ETF has a P/E ratio of 26x, which is higher than both BABA's TTM non-GAAP P/E at 18.59x and forward non-GAAP P/E at 20.9x.

Further, even though the ETF's single biggest holding is in fact Alibaba, BABA has the smallest forward P/E after Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) among the biggest five holdings of the ETF. SSNLF trades at 9.59x, while the remaining three, Alphabet (GOOGL), Oracle (ORCL) and Tesla (TSLA) are at 25.38x, 48.22x and 255.94x respectively. The average of these four biggest holdings is at a high of 84.78x, suggesting that there could potentially be a premium on BABA even now.

  • E-commerce driven valuation

To assess where BABA is with reference to its e-commerce related valuations, we don't need to look farther than the stock's 5y average forward non-GAAP P/E ratio. The number stands at 14.05x, which of course is far lower than the present level of 20.9x.

The TTM GAAP P/E fares better, at 19.06x. In the past year, it has risen to over 28x, which could technically suggest upside. But as the chart below shows, the stock tends not to stay at over 20x levels. For much of the past year at least, it has ranged between 15-20x. This gives little room for a sustained upside on the stock.

  • The fair valuation

The above discussion indicates that while the potential for AI can raise the stock's fair valuation, even until recently, BABA has been guided more by historical valuations, that correlate better with its e-commerce business.

To reconcile these differences, a weighted average approach is taken to determine the fair valuation. The weight of the AI and cloud business is the contribution of the segment's adjusted EBIT21A, which, as earlier noted is at 7.6%. The remainder is ascribed to the e-commerce segment and all others.

The averages valuation of the cloud business is taken as the average the market multiples for the four biggest holdings under AIQ after BABA. For e-commerce, BABA's historical 5y average forward non-GAAP P/E suffices. This results a fair P/E ratio of 25.76x, which implies that there's still 20% upside to the stock.

The risks

There are however, some risks to consider too. These are as follows:

  • Massive investments in a competitive market

The first is the amount of investments that the company plans in AI at over USD 50 billion, which was its initial plan. Since the company hasn't revealed the amount it will now spend, let's just consider here that it sticks to its previous spending amount. This is over 19% of the company's total assets. With just 11.2% in long-term debt as a proportion of asset right now, this sounds like a risk that can be absorbed.

However, it's only so if the investments pay off. The competition in the AI market is heating up, with companies developing models around the world. While the US and China lead the race right now, there's no guarantee that they will remain ahead, which can compromise returns on any investments. In the worst case, if the value of these investments needs to be written off or written down at some point, the company's earnings can suffer. This brings me to the next point.

  • China e-commerce's EBITA contraction

The latest signs from the company's domestic e-commerce segment aren't encouraging, with an EBITA contraction of 21% YoY in Q1 FY26. The company explains that this is due to its investments, among other segments, in Taobao Instant Commerce, which as the name suggests, provides quick deliveries.

But it remains to be seen whether these investment will pay off, tying this point back to the earlier one on the risk involved, even if this spending is necessary. If the segment didn't bring much of the profits, it wouldn't matter as much. But since it does, it becomes more crucial that they do, at a time that the EBITA is shrinking.

What next?

For now though, it's too early to see big investments and their risk of a negative impact as substantial. The verdict is tilted more in favour of the potential of the opportunity now.

Based on this the estimated fair market multiple implies that the Buy rating on BABA stays put. Even if the argument were made that the comparable AI stocks are overvalued, the fact remains that this is a story that will play out over the next few years. And by 2027, BABA's P/E starts looking improved even considering the historical metrics.

So, medium-to-long-term investors can still benefit from an investment in BABA, even if short-term fluctuations can't be ruled out based on on any challenging news while the AI story is still developing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Corporación América Airports: Don't Get Shaken Out By Political Uncertainty
data.description:
Argentina has been in the news recently. That's primarily because the country has its midterm legislative elections coming up in October.

If President Javier Milei's party fares well, this would help give him a mandate to continue enacting his aggressive pro-business reform agenda. If his party were to lose in the upcoming elections, however, he would risk becoming a so-called lame duck president who would have little power for the remainder of his term.

Earlier in September, some provincial elections in Buenos Aires went against Milei's party by a wider than expected margin. Investors interpreted that as a sign that the October elections could also come up short of expectations. That said, the median national Argentine voter is considerably more conservative than the residents of the Buenos Aires area. Overall, prediction markets such as Kalshi and Polymarket continue to have Milei's party as slight favorites to win the October legislative elections.

Regardless, investors assumed the worst, with Argentine bonds and stocks absolutely collapsing up through last Friday. Several Argentine equities were down more than 50% year-to-date, the country's sovereign bonds had plummeted, and the country's risk premium (measured by country default swaps) was skyrocketing.

That changed on Monday. At that time, U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent wrote on X, the social media platform formerly known as Twitter, that the U.S. was prepared to stand by its ally Argentina and that various options were on the table to support the Argentine economy. On Tuesday, Bessent and President Trump met with Milei.

And on Wednesday, Bessent further commented on X that the U.S. was prepared to provide up to $20 billion in swap lines along with buying the Argentine government's U.S. Dollar-denominated bonds.

Not surprisingly, this led to a major rebound in Argentine bonds and equities. The Peso also regained much of its recent losses. Still, Argentine asset prices remain far below where they were earlier in 2025.

Here's a year-to-date chart of various Argentine equities that are listed in New York:

You'll notice that one of these is not like the others. That outlier would be Corporación América Airports S.A. (NYSE:CAAP), one of my top holdings and my continued preferred way to get exposure to the Argentine market. Let's update the thesis in light of the recent political jitters.

Corporación América Airports: An Argentine Equity With Less FX Volatility

I first covered CAAP stock back in October 2020 when shares were trading around $2/share. The stock has rallied almost tenfold since then, and this is my eighth public article on the company here at Seeking Alpha. So, I'd refer back to my prior work for the ins and outs of the business model, growth prospects, and details around capital allocation.

With today's update, however, it's worth zooming in on political risk for Corporación América Airports now that Argentina's finances and political system are at the center of considerable uncertainty.

If you read my past articles about CAAP shares, one of my frequent complaints was that the market wasn't assigning any premium to the company as compared to the overall basket of Argentine stocks. Traders seemed to be buying Argentine equities indiscriminately without paying much heed to the actual business fundamentals at each company. That works well in a bull market; as long as Milei's reforms were on track, everything with a ticker in Argentina kept going up.

2025 has been far more complicated, however. Milei's initial success in bringing down inflation is already accounted for and now voters are tiring of austerity and Argentina's continued lukewarm economic performance. Taming inflation is one thing, but people still want rising incomes and purchasing power as well.

Meanwhile, Milei's efforts to stabilize the exchange rate of the Argentine Peso have caused the country's foreign reserves to start to run down. Foreign investors got cold feet and have unceremoniously exited their longs, with many Argentine stocks being down 30% or more year-to-date as of this writing.

CAAP is finally standing apart from the pack; its shares are up slightly year-to-date despite the nationwide sell-off. This is the sort of macroeconomic climate that makes its unique features shine.

For one, airport traffic is relatively agnostic to political developments. Sure, travel is cyclical to a considerable degree. But it's a lot less prone to political meddling than industries like banking, mining, or energy. A socialist government, should one come back into power in Argentina in the near future, is unlikely to single out infrastructure operators for particularly burdensome regulation.

In fact, we have a great example of this. Corporación América Airports was struggling during the pandemic with most of its operating airports being temporarily shut down due to the lockdowns. There were concerns that the company, which at that point had a heavily levered balance sheet, could end up teetering toward insolvency. The stock price was trading around $2.

At that point, Argentina's government, which was then headed by a left-wing president, extended CAAP's Argentina airport concessions by 10 years, without any upfront monetary cost, in order to provide the operator with pandemic relief.

It's my contention that regardless of the political ideology of the government, everyone wants a well-functioning system of airports that serve to draw tourists and promote commerce within Argentina. Additionally, there is generally not the same sort of environmentalist or anti-capitalist sentiments against airports that you'd find toward industries like oil production or gold mining.

Additionally, CAAP's contract with the Argentine government includes guaranteed returns on invested capital. So, the government can't penalize or reduce CAAP's returns from its Argentine assets without being in breach of contract and potentially facing legal consequences.

To that point, the contract also gives CAAP something that few other Argentine companies have access to: Guaranteed U.S. Dollar revenues. For all commercial international passengers arriving at CAAP's Argentine Category 1 airports on flights of at least 188 miles, CAAP gets paid 57 U.S. Dollars.

CAAP only charges in Argentine Pesos for passengers flying domestic Argentine routes, where it gets 5,685 pesos ($3.92) each. Domestic is a small fraction of the overall Argentine traffic market, however, and CAAP's fee is so low on these domestic routes as to be insignificant within its overall business economics. If the peso devalues sharply going forward, it would have minimal negative impact on the business as the lion's share of revenues is from those international passengers who pay $57 each regardless of what happens with the exchange rate. Given that CAAP has labor and other expenses in pesos, it may actually benefit from some devaluation of the local currency.

Additionally, CAAP is hardly a pure-play bet on Argentina.

As per the company's Q2 results shown below, Argentina accounts for just 54% of total revenues:

I think people sleep on the company's international operations because none of its other markets, in isolation, looks that large. Armenia is about 15% of revenues and no other country accounts for more than 10%. However, there are a lot of other countries there that do participate. In aggregate, the non-Argentine assets are 46% of the business. And some of those markets, like Italy, are fairly solid travel geographies which should be rewarded with a higher EBITDA multiple than Argentina.

Even assuming a seeming worst case for investors where Milei's economic reform agenda fails and the country undoes its recent progress in stamping out inflation, there's little reason to believe the wheels would fall off of CAAP's business. Travel will still continue to and from Argentina and those passengers will pay their airport use fees in dollars. And nearly half of the company's overall revenues don't come from Argentine airports in the first place.

Furthermore, Corporación América Airports is actively in negotiations to add to its airport portfolio with bidding underway in Montenegro, Iraq, and Angola. With CAAP's net debt to EBITDA falling to just 1.0x recently, I believe the firm is now under-levered compared to where management intends to run it over the longer term. This makes additional airport acquisitions likely. Perhaps the company will initiate a dividend as well, as its balance sheet continues to strengthen.

CAAP Stock Bottom Line

Corporación América Airports is firing on all cylinders. Overall traffic growth accelerated back into double-digit territory in August, growing 10.2% year-over-year.

Impressively, Argentina in particular continues to deliver double-digit passenger count increases despite the slowdown in Argentina's economy over the past few months. If the recent U.S. aid to Argentina provides some stimulus to the Argentine economy, CAAP's momentum could pick up given how strong the Argentine travel market has remained despite the recent macroeconomic wobble.

I'm not the only one remaining upbeat on Corporación América Airports despite the troublesome Argentine political headlines. Earlier in September, Itau maintained its outperform rating on CAAP stock and assigned a year-end 2026 price target for CAAP stock of $23.80 per share.

I liked what I saw in the company's Q2 earnings. And the company's most recent conference call had an upbeat tone with the firm reporting positive news on many fronts.

As for the Argentine drama that is currently clouding the macro picture, that could go either way. For what it's worth, I bought a speculative position in the Argentine banking sector recently. And with the U.S. coming to Argentina's aid economically, that should improve the odds that Milei will be able to navigate through the current fraught situation. That said, it's quite possible that the elections do not have an investor-friendly outcome. I'd be a seller on most Argentine assets, including the banks, if Milei's economic reforms grind to a halt.

However, as I've laid out in this article, I believe CAAP is the exception to that. Even if Argentina does not remain on its current economic trajectory, CAAP's combination of dollar revenues, diversification outside of Argentina, and focus on a non-politically sensitive business sector should allow it to thrive regardless of who governs Argentina going forward.

The company's operating results continue to be tremendous. As a result, I am ignoring the political noise and sticking with a strong buy rating on CAAP stock today.

data.title: 3 Reasons Tesla Could Be Worth $600 Per Share By The End Of 2027 (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the last few years, we’ve put out a number of articles on Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA), where we’ve come out as bulls or bears on the stock depending on market conditions.

In November of 2023, we initiated coverage with a Sell rating when the company looked expensive, followed by an upgrade in April of 2024 when the selloff looked overdone.

In January of this year, we thought TSLA looked prohibitively expensive - even taking into account the company's Robotaxis potential - and soon after, shares plunged lower as Musk’s political entanglements and broader pressure in the automotive industry put a damper on profits.

More recently, we've upgraded shares to a Hold, arguing that the company appears fairly valued by the market, based on its current businesses and the potential value of a fully operational Robotaxi segment:

While some view the company as a preposterously expensive car manufacturer, we believe that upcoming automated ride hail service could be incredibly valuable for society, and incredibly profitable for shareholders.

Since our most recent rating, there have been a number of positive developments at Tesla that we think are worth covering. When combined, we think they're enough to upgrade our view on the company from neutral to bullish.

Today, we'll outline the company's recent developments, give our outlook for the stock, and make the case that it is once again time to pile into this advanced manufacturing juggernaut.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Reason 1: Profit Inflection

The first positive development that we haven’t covered in previous articles is the company's Q2 earnings, which we thought were particularly strong.

While revenues were down roughly 12% YoY versus Q2 in 2024, and operating margins were down a little more than 200 basis points, we were encouraged by the fact that the company's gross margins and operating margins improved sequentially from recent quarters:

If you recall, Tesla has seen significant margin pressure in recent quarters as sales volumes have dropped against a relatively fixed cost base. This fixed cost base provides the company significant operational leverage when volumes and revenues are strong, but with musk's political entanglements throughout the first half of this year, alongside a price slashing campaign led by Chinese manufacturers, it led to a significant drop in quarterly margins for the company.

Now, with Q2 margins moving in the right direction on a QoQ basis, it’s possible to make the case profits have inflected back up and should continue to do so going forward. As prices continue to normalize following a discount heavy 2023 and 2024, it seems as though Tesla could be in a good position to grow earnings-per-share from here.

Another positive was the firm's service segment, which grew 17% YoY. While the segment sports lower gross profitability versus the automotive segment on a nominal basis, the growth is welcome, and this boosted quarterly net incomes.

We expect this will continue, as the company's supercharger network continues to expand, preowned inventory continues to turn over, and the in-house insurance business finds its footing.

Taking together, we’d argue that Tesla is in a materially stronger financial position now then it was earlier in the year, which is a positive

Reason 2: Musk’s Pay Package

On September 12, Tesla announced a $1 trillion pay package for CEO Elon Musk.

While large, the package is contingent on performance goals, and we believe this is a massive net positive for shareholders.

Many (including the Pope) have been quick to write this off as being gratuitous or uneconomical, but we have a different view.

Over the last few years, Elon Musk’s interest in Tesla has seemed to wane, as the company's CEO has found his attention split across multiple new ventures, including X (formally Twitter), SpaceX (SPACE), Neuralink, and a foray into politics. Since 2022, shareholders have bemoaned the lack of focus from Elon on the company, and this has notably been a period during which TSLA's share price has been stagnant.

While we're not a fan of the way that Elon Musk went about earning this pay package - i.e., complaining that he didn’t have enough of a stake in the company - there’s no question that these payouts will re-incentivize musk to get on the same page as the company's shareholders.

Here are the details.

By meeting various market cap and production targets, Musk will earn 423 million additional shares of Tesla, representing 12% of the current outstanding shares of the company.

The share vesting is split into 12 separate tranches, the first of which unlocks after Tesla reaches $2 trillion in market cap.

From there, Musk will earn an additional tranche for every $500 billion in market cap gained, up to a cap of $8.5 trillion.

The package also has various production targets, including 20 million vehicles, 20 million active full self driving subscriptions, 1 million Optimus robots, and 1 million Robotaxis in commercial use.

Finally, this pay package aligns Musk for the next 7 1/2 to 10 years, over which any earned trenches will fully vest. This demonstrably links Musk - arguably the company's greatest asset - with shareholder interests.

Of note, during negotiations with the board, Musk gave his assurances that he would wind down his political involvement and discuss succession planning.

While Elon's promises have a mixed track record - just to ask anyone who expected full self driving in 2019 - we feel as though Musk's politically active (and volatile) period is likely coming to an end.

Finally, for those who feel as though the package is overly generous to Musk, we would make the argument that if Tesla earns a market cap $8.5 trillion, it will have produced an absolutely unheard of amount of economic value for shareholders at society writ large.

As a result, we feel the compensation package materially boosts our investment thesis.

Season 3: Robotaxi Progress

The third and final reason that we feel confident once again getting bullish on Tesla has to do with how much progress the company has made since initially launching Robotaxis in Austin earlier this year.

We have followed the coverage of this service very closely, and it appears as though many think Tesla’s taxi service is safe, efficient, and well run.

Obviously, having a Tesla safety monitor in the car is a bit of a downer with respect to the eventual promise of Robotaxis and privacy, but it makes sense with respect to the broader roll-out goals.

In order to win regulatory approval to launch the Robotaxi service in new jurisdictions, the company needs to prove the service's safety and reliability. For now, having safety monitors in the car makes it easier to diagnose and fix problems, leading to a faster learning - and thus, adoption - curve.

Speaking of regulatory approval, the company recently won clearance from the Arizona Department of transportation to begin testing Robotaxi vehicles in the state, which we view as a massive win.

In our previous article on Tesla, where we compared its Robotaxi service with Google’s (GOOG) Waymo, we argued that Waymo appeared to be in a better position, given that it was in more markets and had way more miles driven.

As Tesla begins winning more approvals, we expect this Waymo lead to evaporate. Recently, on the Q2 earnings call, Musk mentioned that the company was in the process of getting regulatory permission to launch in the Bay Area, Nevada and Florida - in addition to Arizona - alongside a number of "other places":

And we're getting the regulatory permission to launch in the Bay Area, Nevada, Arizona, Florida, and a number of other places. So as we get the approvals and we prove out safety, then we will be launching autonomous ride-hailing in most of the country. And I think we'll probably have autonomous ride-hailing in probably half the population of the U.S. by the end of the year. That's at least our goal, subject to regulatory approvals. I think we'll technically be able to do it. So assuming we get regulatory approvals, it's probably addressing half of the population in the U.S. by the end of the year.

If these launches go well, then we expect that Tesla could launch in a number of metros nationwide by the end of the year, and potentially nationally by the end of 2026.

This growth trajectory would come in well above our previous bull case estimates, indicating that the fair value of Robotaxis (and Tesla on the whole) could be higher than previously anticipated.

Taken together, over the next five years, analysts are anticipating Tesla to grow EPS to $13.94 per share. This represents massive continued YoY EPS growth off of the back of Robotaxi execution and a budding Optimus business:

While neither of these businesses exist right now, Musk is no stranger to an execution challenge, and recent progress on this front leaves us optimistic.

In the event that Tesla can execute this growth path, then it appears possible that shares of Tesla could reach $600 or more by the end of 2027:

This model bakes in multiple contraction from a P/E of 225x (where we are now) to a P/E of 190 over the next 26 months, based on the fact that continued execution on the growth opportunity would likely keep the multiple inflated. It's likely we'd see some compression, but not to a drastic degree. For as long as we can remember, TSLA has been valued at these levels.

Simply put, if TSLA can achieve what analysts - and we - think it can over the next five years, then it appears as though there is considerable upside for investors.

Hence our rating upgrade.

How We're Wrong

At this point, Tesla is ultimately a 'show me' story. That is, the company needs to execute on the growth opportunity, which comes with significant technological challenges and product hurdles. If the company stumbles or loses momentum, especially with respect to regulatory progress on the Robotaxis, then shares are likely worth materially less than they are today.

This brings us to the other risk - valuation.

Again, as we’ve laid out, one can make a logical case for investing in Tesla, based on the current multiple and Robotaxi growth prospects, even with some multiple compression.

However, if the execution stumbles, it’s likely that the market will re-rate shares lower. This is a key risk simply due to how expensive the company is right now, especially vs. peers.

Make no mistake. There are significant risks to investing in Tesla at today’s price.

Summary

Overall, we think Tesla deserves to be upgraded to a Buy based on recent profitability progress in the core automotive segment, a newly "aligned" pay package with CEO Musk, and continued momentum on the automated ride hail front.

While there are risks associated with a purchase of Tesla's stock at today’s price, we think there is also material upside in the coming years if the company can execute.

Cheers¬

data.title: Prudent Financial Management And Operational Leverage Strengthens Astronics' Bull Case
data.description:
Astronics Corporation (ATRO) is on a stellar run this year, having already seen its share price rise over 170% YTD. Volatility has accompanied these gains, but every dip has been bought by investors, helping the stock continue its uptrend. The company designs and manufactures products used in the aerospace and defense industry. While a quarter of the company’s revenue comes from the Defense & Government segment, it sets itself apart from the rest through its dominance in providing cabin infrastructure to the commercial aerospace industry. If you have travelled by air, there is a good chance you have interacted with Astronics products through the in-flight entertainment (IFE) systems, in-seat power systems (ISPS), and cabin lighting and avionics. This segment powers more than two-thirds of the company’s revenue and also drives my bullish thesis on the stock.

I am optimistic on the company’s prospects not just because of its financial resurgence but also because of the way it has positioned itself to benefit from emerging air travel trends. It is set to be a major beneficiary of a potential overhaul of in-flight entertainment systems and the replacement of older aircraft with new-generation ones over the course of the next two decades. The firm’s focus on adding to its defense capabilities also bodes well at a time when defense budgets are going up across the globe.

BYOD Trend to Spur Investments

Air travel demand took 4 years to return to pre-COVID levels and continues to thrive post-recovery. As more and more people travel by air, airlines are fighting to win passengers by improving cabin infrastructure. This spending is driven partly by passenger preferences, as people prefer bringing their own devices to the aircraft rather than relying on in-flight entertainment systems.

I believe there is a good chance that most airlines will remove backseat screens in the not-too-distant future. American Airlines started this trend over 8 years ago and received mixed responses from both customers and peers. It didn’t stop them, and by early 2026, they are likely to remove the backseat screens from their Legacy Airbus A319s as well. In 2020, Etihad Airlines removed the backseat screens from 23 of its aircraft, while two years later, Malaysia Airlines did something similar with its Boeing 737-800s. In all these cases, the common denominator was the fact that a vast majority of the people brought their own entertainment devices to the plane. These airlines decided to remove the redundant screens, thus removing unnecessary weight from the aircraft while also saving on the maintenance expenses of keeping these screens functional.

Satellite-based Wi-Fi services like Starlink can now provide high-speed internet, enabling passengers to stream on their own devices rather than rely on the IFE. This aligns with the Bring Your Own Devices (BYOD) trend. I believe passengers will prefer streaming on their own devices if given the opportunity. 65% of the passengers already prefer using their own devices or in-flight entertainment purposes, according to a study by Tech Sci Research, as quoted recently by Simple Flying on why airlines do not want to spend on backseat screens anymore.

I must add here that this trend is more likely to be adopted on short-haul flights using narrow body aircraft, where passengers are more likely to sacrifice on in-flight entertainment options to save money, and airlines are more concerned about reducing costs. Astronics has an equal revenue distribution between narrow-body and wide-body aircraft, so it benefits from this potential change. As this trend materializes, the modern personal devices will demand quality infrastructure, including consistent power and connectivity. This is where Astronics comes in. It provides the right infrastructure to power this BYOD trend. The company controls more than 90% market share in In-Seat Power Systems! Even when backseat screens are phased out, ATRO stays relevant as personal entertainment systems still need power to run. In fact, they need an even more reliable power infrastructure, making this shift a positive catalyst for the company.

In addition to a reliable power source, BYOD will also require a reliable Wi-Fi system. Astronics was the first company to bring Wi-Fi 6E-enabled cabin wireless Access Point (CWAP) to aircraft through its product CABINAXe. CABINAXe not only solves the problem of sharing bandwidth between closely spaced passengers but also operates at a spectrum that does not interfere with the aircraft’s own communication systems. When airlines opt for internet service providers like Starlink, they need a reliable way to distribute that high-speed internet among passengers in a confined space. CABINAXe helps achieve exactly that.

The company generated 51% of its sales from the In-Flight Entertainment & Connectivity (IFEC) segment. As passenger behavior shifts towards a more connected experience, this segment will continue to be the company’s primary growth engine.

Benefitting From Growth Across the Fleet

Another factor that helps fuel spending on cabin infrastructure is aircraft innovation. According to Airbus, 34% of the current global fleet consists of new-generation aircraft. These offer 25% better fuel efficiency and lower carbon emissions. I have already pointed out above that airlines care about making short-haul flights cheaper, so a 25% fuel efficiency is a big deal. The money thus saved can be diverted toward improving passenger experience without sacrificing margins – something Airlines often do to attract more passengers.

The fact that 34% of the global fleet is new-generation doesn’t directly boost Astronics' revenues; it just points to a new trend, which is significant. The existing global fleet of 24,730 is likely to grow to 49,210 by the end of 2044. This includes 43,420 new deliveries!

In other words, most of the existing older generation of aircraft will be retired over the next two decades, replaced by new-generation aircraft requiring modern infrastructure: infrastructure that Astronics provides. This, together with an anticipated 3.6% compound annual growth rate in global air traffic by 2050, offers the company a perfect opportunity to increase its commercial aerospace revenues.

ATRO derives 68% of its sales from commercial aerospace, which is equally distributed between narrow-body and wide-body aircraft. Wide-body aircraft tend to offer higher content per plane, resulting in better margins. Narrow-body aircraft, which are growing faster than the wide-body versions, tend to offer higher volumes. ATRO benefits from both through its 50/50 revenue distribution between both aircraft types. More importantly, ATRO’s ISPS powers 80% of all the wide-body aircraft globally, thus ensuring it enjoys a large piece of the high-margin pie in In-Seat Power System deployment.

The company’s Q2 financials also show a healthy growth in bookings from $557.3 million in 2021 to $841.8 million by Q2 2025 (TTM). The fact that the backlog has increased at a slightly higher rate shows OEMs like Boeing and Airbus continue to produce at a higher production rate, increasing the demand for cabin infrastructure.

Astronics’ impressive backlog, a strong footprint within the in-flight entertainment niche, and evolving consumer preferences in the commercial aerospace segment all make its business fundamentals very promising. These factors endorse the management’s recent upward revision of their 2025 revenue guidance to a range of $840 million to $860 million. Moreover, the management has forecasted revenues to reach $956.5 million by 2028, which I find very realistic given many of the recent developments. What also makes me optimistic about ATRO’s projections is the potential opportunities for their defense operations, which I will discuss in detail.

Focus on Defense Capabilities

We are witnessing a widespread increase in global defense spending, which has soared to unprecedented levels. Global military expenditure in 2024 stood at roughly $2.5 trillion, marking a steep rise across Europe and the United States. The “Golden Dome” defense system suggested by President Donald Trump could spur immense spending in the sector. With an estimated cost of $175 billion up to project initiation in 2029, followed by an anticipated expenditure of $831 billion over the next 2 decades, the aerospace and defense industry players will garner attention in the years to come. This will surely extend to OEMs like Astronics, who provide critical communication systems, safety components, and test equipment to large manufacturers.

On that front, the U.S. Army’s Future Long-Range Assault Aircraft (FLRAA) program also carries a lot of significance. This program aims to enhance the military’s existing combat capabilities by replacing roughly 2,000 black hawk helicopters in the coming years. Costs associated with this program are estimated at around $70 billion.

In fact, during the Jefferies Mining and Industrials Conference on September 3, 2025, the company reiterated its stance on mission-critical defense systems. Astronics would leverage its modern electronic circuit breaker capabilities that will replace the more traditional thermal mechanical breaker systems. Most notably, Astronics is tied to a deal with aerospace manufacturer Bell Textron, which has further strengthened the long-standing partnership between the two entities. Under the terms of this deal, Astronics will supply advanced power systems and solutions for Bell’s aircraft fleet, including Bell 525, Bell 360 Invictus, and V-280 Valor, through Astronics CorePower® solutions. Chairman, President & CEO Peter Gundermann highlighted the company’s $90 million development spend that began in 2024. According to initial forecasts, the company will end up extending circa $90 million by 2026, which would also mark the first flight of the FLRAA program.

The above graph shows that Defense & Government contracts contributed 22% to ATRO’s revenue stream during the 12 months ending in June 2025. The company’s overall revenue mix is more tilted towards its commercial aerospace operations, which have been contributing close to 70% to revenues, on average. Trends in defense spending highlighted above present an opportunity for ATRO management to further diversify their revenue mix and curtail their over-reliance on the commercial aerospace segment. By increasing the proportionate revenues from the defense segment, ATRO could also mitigate risks associated with commercial aerospace operations that are sensitive to consumers’ evolving preferences.

Envoy Aerospace Acquisition

Astronics Corporation announced its acquisition of Envoy Aerospace in July 2025, penning down a deal for $8 million. Envoy Aerospace is a highly reputable Organization Designation Authorization (ODA) services provider. Envoy Aerospace helps clients attain U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) certifications, and is composed of a team of professionals who will now join the Astronics team.

This collaboration would add Envoy’s existing customers to Astronics' portfolio. The collective customer base will benefit from advanced ODA services to obtain Supplemental Type Certificates (STCs) and Parts Manufacturer Approvals (PMAs), as per FAA requirements. It is important to highlight that there is a shortage of such ODA services in the industry, and hence, this acquisition provides Astronics with a competitive edge.

Financials

In the first half of the year, ATRO reported $410.6 million in revenue, a 7% YoY jump, keeping the company on track for its 2025 revenue projections of $820 million to $860 million. Sales growth was driven by a strong backlog of $599.2 million entering into 2025, and was predominantly concentrated across functions within the aerospace segment.

Cost of products sold was $296.9 million, resulting in approximately 28% gross margins for the first six months. However, it is important to note that such high gross margins resulted from a change in the presentation of R&D expenses in the first quarter of 2025. These costs were previously part of the costs of products sold, which resulted in lower gross profits. The company now presents them as a separate line item under the gross profits.

Operating profits for the first half of 2025 stood at $17.9 million, a 94% YoY increase compared with $9.2 million during the first half of 2024.
