Such expansion of operating profits came on the back of higher operating leverage achieved by the company, backed by strong volumetric growth which drove the topline upwards. It also led to a net income of around $11 million for the first half, completing the company’s turnaround after multiple years of losses. Stability in supply chain functions and process automations to cut labor costs were the key drivers behind the operating leverage of 53% for the aerospace segment. It is important to note that the company did not incur any major capital expenditure to achieve the purpose. A larger proportion of fixed costs magnified the profitability ratios for the aerospace segment, which contributes more than 90% to overall sales.

Balance sheet liquidity continues to be a strong point for Astronics, as current assets stand at 65% of total assets. Through effective balance sheet management, the company was also able to bring down its net interest expense to $6.2 million compared to $11.6 million during the first half of 2024. ATRO has also managed its working capital changes, excluding cash & marketable securities, very efficiently throughout 2024 and the first half of 2025. This, along with higher operating leverage and volumetric growth, facilitated a turnaround in the operating cash flow position as well, which had remained in the negative territory in the prior 3 years.

Astronics' unlevered cash flow position has also been solidifying in recent quarters, providing solid grounds for investors to stay optimistic. The company currently has $43.4 million of unlevered FCF on a TTM basis, which marks a significant improvement in the coverage ratio. Going forward, such financial discipline is a necessity given that ATRO carries over $159 million in long-term debt and close to $31.5 million in long-term operating lease liabilities on its balance sheet.

Another recent favorable development relates to ATRO’s $165 million convertible notes issued at the backend of 2024. There was a widespread belief among market participants regarding the potential dilution risk associated with these 5.5% 2030 notes. In share count terms, it amounted to an additional 7.2 million shares. During the second week of September, ATRO management carried out a successful refinancing of these convertible notes. As ATRO registered a more than 170% jump in share price during 2025, management viewed these 5.5% 2030 notes to be deep in the money. Hence, they decided to repurchase 80% of the notes, i.e. $132 million worth of principal, at a price of $285 million. Hence, the dilution risk relating to the 2030 notes is now limited to just 1.4 million shares.

This purchase was financed through 3 sources, i.e. $11 million cash, $85 million borrowings from an interest-bearing Asset-Based Revolver (ABR) facility, and the rest from a new $225 million 0% 2031 convertible notes issue. These notes are non-interest-bearing and carry an initial conversion price of $54.87, which represents a 25% conversion premium.

Valuation

ATRO has been a star performer this year with more than 170% YTD gain, comfortably beating both the S&P 500’s (SPY) 16% gain and the SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF’s (XAR) 39% gain. Among individual stocks, I have identified three peers namely Collins Aerospace, a subsidiary of RTX Corporation (RTX), Docummun Incorporated (DCO), and TransDigm Group Incorporated (TDG).

Since the In-Seat Power System is where Astronics dominates, this is where I will begin the comparison. Collins Aerospace is a company that has a strong overlap with Astronics in this segment. However, Astronics serves over 290 airlines and specializes in providing this infrastructure, focusing on flexibility and passenger preferences. While Collins Aerospace also does the same, it is less dominant. Also, Collins Aerospace is focused on leveraging the scale of RTX and has bigger fish to fry, especially in installing mission-critical systems. Astronics therefore does not have to worry about protecting its market share from its closest competitor. Collins Aerospace dominates the in-flight entertainment market, again leveraging the scale of RTX to bundle its products with other systems that it installs in passenger planes. Since I have already pointed out that in-flight entertainment systems could move from backseat screens to individual devices, resulting in a greater demand for ISPS, a segment that Astronics already dominates, there is again no danger of losing market share.

As for the other two peers, TDG and DCO, there are many similarities in the way their business is structured. TDG has a similar revenue mix, generating 60% of its revenues from commercial aerospace. For DCO, the same number stands at 40%. Astronics is more skewed toward the commercial aerospace segment as a percentage of revenue at 68%, but these businesses do not directly compete in each other’s market share. For instance, DCO only provides certain components necessary in the lighting or power systems within the cabin; it doesn’t have a product like Astronics does. TDG is a holding company and owns smaller companies that provide components that go into in-seat power systems. However, it again doesn’t compete with the type of infrastructure that Astronics provides. Despite this, these two companies are the two closest in terms of where they stand in the aerospace industry, so they offer a good comparison when determining Astronics' valuation.

Astronics has comfortably outperformed both DCO and TDG since the beginning of the year, as shown below.

This outperformance has helped the stock close its valuation gap with these peers, which means it is no longer as undervalued as it was earlier in the year. On a forward P/E basis, Astronics, with a multiple of 26.0,9 has already surpassed DCO at 25.18 but still has some way to reach TDG’s P/E ratio of 35.15. The Price/Sales of 1.83 has similarly surpassed DCO’s 1.75 though just like in the case of P/E ratio, it still has a long way to go to catch up to TDG’s 8.80 multiple.

The tricky question in this scenario is not whether the momentum for Astronics stock will continue, but whether the stock is a buy at the current price. It is trading well above its 50-day moving average, and judging by the strong recovery after the most recent convertible notes offering, as well as earnings, I am inclined to think waiting for a pullback is not a smart move.

If one buys the stock at the current market price, the question that remains then is what is the upside from current levels?

I have used different forward-looking multiples to estimate a target price range for Astronics. Since a price hike in shares during 2025, Astronics is currently trading at a 1.83x forward price-to-sales multiple. After management’s upward revision in the 2025 annual sales forecast between $840 million to $860 million, the target price works out to be above $44 a share. Similarly, the price target based on 2025 EPS estimates of $1.64 turns out to be $42.79, assuming a 26.09x forward P/E ratio. However, as we can see the stock is currently trading very close to these estimates, especially following the refinancing through 2031 0% convertible notes.

Hence, I added more rigour to my analysis by applying the same P/E multiple of 26.09x to 2026 EPS estimate of $2.01. This results in a price target of $52.44 by the end of 2026, which is very close to the conversion price of recently issued convertible notes.

Risks

The risk profile of Astronics appears limited, but I have identified three factors that investors must know before taking exposure in the stock. The first one is litigation. In February 2025, Astronics was asked to pay up by a UK court as part of the Astronics vs. Lufthansa Technik Case. The amount is just below $12 million and well covered by the company’s cash flows, even when legal fees and other expenses are added to it. The lawsuit in France is still pending, but is unlikely to cause any major headache. A favorable result in one jurisdiction is not a strong enough reason to influence a case in another jurisdiction; such is the nature of lawsuits in the aerospace industry. The litigation risk, therefore, is minimal.

The second risk relates to the recently issued 2031 0% convertible notes for the refinancing of a previous issue in 2024. Although the conversion price of $54.87 offers more favorable terms to existing shareholders, the shareholding dilution risk still exists. Astronics has made an announcement to buy $25 million worth of capped calls to mitigate such dilution risks in the future. The strike price associated with those capped calls is $83.41, which offers a significant amount of protection from dilution risk. But at the same time, it poses refinancing risk to Astronics in case the share price does not rise enough. Hence, investors should closely monitor movements in the share price in the upcoming quarter.

Finally, I would like to highlight risks associated with Astronics' operating leverage. As mentioned earlier, operating leverage has played a pivotal role in magnifying profitability ratios for Astronics in the recent quarters. But it also comes with a potential downside, as the ratios might take a bigger hit in case sales growth is lackluster. This is the reason why Astronics is in need of consistent operational and financial discipline to build upon the current turnaround. Again, it will be interesting to see the financial disclosures in the upcoming quarters that will provide more context on the effectiveness of management’s operational strategy.

Recommendation

With a solid demand backlog and an impressive financial turnaround, I feel very optimistic about Astronics. Share price has delivered over 170% returns on a YTD basis. The company is on course to turn profitable in 2025, which will strengthen investor confidence and provide support to the share price. Despite the highlighted risks, prospects for Astronics look good as the company is undergoing a financial and operational turnaround. Fleet upgrade plans within commercial and military aerospace markets offer encouragement, where Astronics can build upon its existing long-standing business relationships. Based on these factors, I will rate Astronics a Buy.

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs: Political Risk At Its Finest
data.description:
Over the years, I have been following various businesses that depend heavily on political decisions. This is especially true for companies operating in the Energy and Basic Materials sectors, where decisions made by different governments often have strong implications for shareholder returns.

As dependent as companies operating in these industries are on political factors, however, their management teams do their best to avoid fixating on the outcome of certain decisions as the sole factor of success or demise of their companies.

In the case of Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (NYSE:CLF) we see this rhetoric being dialed up, and it appears that shareholders should be anticipating a positive outcome of a policy or government decision each and every quarter. That was one of the reasons why I turned Neutral on the stock a long time ago and just recently said that it's not worth it to try to call the bottom in the CLF share price.

Since I last covered CLF and laid out my reasoning why trying to call the bottom was not worth the effort, the stock is now up by more than 66%. To some short-term speculators this might seem like an attractive opportunity, but recent performance simply confirms my thesis that CLF has become a stock that is way too speculative for long-term investors to consider.

The first reason for that is quite simple - volatility. Even though CLF is up by more than 66% in a matter of months, the stock still underperformed its major competitors in the U.S. over the past 5-year period. Not only that, but the performance gap is very wide and even without calculating the variance of daily returns, it is obvious that CLF stock has been more volatile than those of Nucor Corporation (NUE) and Steel Dynamics, Inc. (STLD).

Consequently, CLF remains priced at a significant discount to both NUE and STLD, which by itself means that investors betting on a turnaround of CLF could expect significant upside.

The last reported quarter in July gave those optimists hope that CLF might be finally turning the corner, as results came slightly above analysts' estimates.

Although this is certainly a possibility, to me the last reported quarter simply confirmed my thesis that too much emphasis is being put on political decisions and that CLF would most likely continue to underperform its competitors over the long term.

Tariffs and Trade Restrictions

During the Q2 2025 earnings release, it was the better-than-expected results that caught investors' attention and as a result we saw a strong share price response. Of course, this reaction was also due to the recently implemented tariffs, which are now beginning to bear fruit and should show on Cleveland-Cliffs' bottom line over the coming quarter.

The state of the automotive sector in the U.S. is one of the key factors that would play a role in CLF's future performance. That is why the investor presentation during the quarter was heavily focused on tariffs and their impact on demand.

For example, more domestically produced light vehicles are anticipated to support demand for automotive steel and allow CLF to exert its pricing power in the sector.

Tariffs implemented on Brazilian pig iron imports are also expected to give CLF certain competitive advantages over some of its peers as the company now relies on its HBI plant in Toledo.

Other relevant news in trade enforcement is the very important 50% tariff that will go into place on Brazilian pig iron, starting August 1. Cleveland-Cliffs does not rely on imported pig iron at all. We have our own hot-briquetted iron facility in Toledo, Ohio, but several of our EAF competitors do rely on important pig iron.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

While these are all good points that investors should consider, it was quite surprising how tariff-dominated the narrative around CLF has become. In addition to everything said above, CLF management has also stressed the importance of Canada implementing trade restrictions—perhaps due to the company's recent acquisition of Stelco, which is geographically exposed to the market.

Our message is very clear and easy to understand. If Prime Minister Carney and his cabinet really want to have a steel industry in Canada, they should put in place significant trade protections. Then they will have a strong domestic steel industry in Canada, able to support a vibrant domestic Canadian market.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

Some comments made about the Chair of the Federal Reserve were a bit surprising and it seems that CLF's success in the automated sector also depends on the appointment of a new Chairman of the Fed.

Once Chairman Jerome Powell is gone, and that's not a matter of when, not if, and as soon as interest rates come down by 50 or 75 basis points, the automotive sector will take off again. Demand is there. But this Fed Chairman will not act. So we need a new Fed Chairman appointed as soon as possible.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

All further supports my thesis that future shareholder returns are increasingly linked to the outcome of very different political decisions—from tariffs implemented on imports in the U.S. to trade restrictions in Canada and even the appointment of a new Chairman of the Fed.

A Turning Point?

Higher realized prices were among the key quantifiable positives during the quarter as many investors have been eagerly awaiting a shift in the downtrend in recent years.

Q2 results were largely driven by better realized pricing, cost reductions and record shipments.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

As expected, the quarterly realized pricing has been linked to the realized gross margins of Cliffs (see the graph below). Unfortunately, the pricing during Q2 2025 was not enough to swing CLF into a positive gross margin territory and based on the historical relationship below we would anticipate a break-even on a gross margin basis at prices of around $1,100 per ton.

CLF's share price, however, is already accounting for a potential improvement in the second half of 2025 as the price-to-sales ratio has moved to levels that we last saw when CLF reported positive gross margins.

Therefore, in order to justify the CLF's current share price, investors should anticipate average quarterly realized prices to continue improving over the coming quarters and at a much higher rate than they did in the first half of 2025.

Even if that does occur, CLF competitors would also enjoy significant profitability tailwinds, while their positive net income figures would act as downside protection in the event of a more adverse event for the U.S. and global economy. That is why I remain optimistic about the performance of some of CLF's major competitors and just recently Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A), (BRK.B) has made a similar bet.

Conclusion

As investors rejoice at the fact that CLF recently delivered better-than-expected results, I remain cautious about the stock's attractiveness due to the significant political risk involved. While CLF cloud certainty outperforms its peers in the near term, doing so consistently over the long run appears unlikely. Moreover, the share price is already accounting for some improvements in the second half of 2025, which, in my view, significantly reduces any upside through the rest of the year.

data.title: Takeda: Excellent Pipeline Kaizen - 6 Drugs, $20 Billion Annually
data.description:
Introduction

Takeda (NYSE:TAK) is currently a unique biopharma case that may lead to opportunities for investors. While the company is facing significant valuation issues, particularly after the costly Shire acquisition, the company has performed deft kaizen to improve their pipeline. In fact, Takeda has quietly developed six phase 3 therapies that have the opportunity to disrupt competition or provide a first-mover advantage in key, profitable niches. Takeda expects to earn up to $20 billion in revenues upon approval and commercialization. These peak sales estimates, included in the following analysis are taken from Takeda’s estimations provided at their JPM 2025 presentation. However, these are lofty figures so I will determine whether they may end up true or not. The key is to analyze each molecule individually, particularly as Takeda has been extremely selective in choosing this pipeline. Let’s take a look.

Zasocitinib

Zasocitinib is a small molecule Tyk2 kinase inhibitor developed using computational chemistry to allow for a new best-in-class psoriasis treatment. This therapy is set to disrupt and outperform current best-in-class inhibitor Sotyktu, which currently earns Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) about $250 million per year as of 2024 and growing at a 40% annual growth rate. As orally administered small molecules, these drugs look to displace the far more costly TNF inhibitors such as AbbVie’s Humira (ABBV) and Amgen’s Enbrel (AMGN) or IL inhibitor mAbs such as Novartis's Cosentyx (NVS). Zasocitinib’s advantage is that the molecule is far more selective, and any advantages in efficacy will be financially supportive, particularly as the company plans to expand into numerous immune indications.

The opportunity is great, but yet to reflect in the share price. One reason for this is that while the selectivity or efficacy shown is “biologic-level”, the huge competition in the immunology market will provide fierce competition that will be a tough battle for debt-burdened Takeda. Basically: do they have the marketing strength to get doctors to prescribe? However, if Takeda succeeds, the $3-$6 billion target will likely be met or exceeded, with IL and TNF inhibitors account for at least $20 billion in sales on their own based on the drugs listed above.

Peak Sales Estimate: $3-$6 billion

Rusfertide

PTG-300, or rusfertide, is a peptide hormone replacement injection used to treat the rare cancer polycythemia vera. As an orphan drug, and first-in-class type of therapy, rusfertide offers both financial and scientific benefits for Takeda. Unmet need and orphan status operational standards apply, i.e. smaller patient populations and reduced trial costs, but higher therapy cost, allowing for investments to be recouped alongside partner Protagonist Therapeutics (PTGX) thanks to their worldwide collaboration and license agreement. Considering the upfront partnership cost was just $300 million for a 50/50 profit share (royalties to PTGX ex-US), blockbuster status will be a boon to Takeda.

An interesting development is the sharply rising share price of PTGX contrasted against the flat price of TAK over the past year or so. Considering the lasting impact of this therapy, the partnership with Protagonist (if Takeda can execute), and the potential success of the peptide therapy modality, I find the value here to be significant. Perhaps an acquisition of PTGX will allow for sales estimates to increase as there will no longer be a 50/50 split.

Peak Sales Estimates: $1-$2 billion, per Takeda management.

Oveporexton

Another first-in-class therapy, oveporexton (TAK-861) is an oral OX2R agonist to treat narcolepsy type 1. This is the first treatment for the disease as current approvals focus merely on dealing with symptoms. Data has been quite strong across all primary and secondary endpoints, and the majority of patients achieved near-normal ranges for measured indicators. Analysts and Takeda expect blockbuster status to be quite achievable, particularly as there is room for expanded indications across all orexin-based disease classes like narcolepsy type 2 and idiopathic hypersomnia. These patients will likely be pleased to switch from current symptom management treatments and into a new, safer modality (no scheduling necessary!).

As an oral small molecule, this means there will be high profit margins, enough to begin tackling the company’s debt issues if approval is earned and commercialization succeeds. However, competition will be fierce, with legacy giant Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ) also developing an orexin-based therapy, and biotech Alkermes (ALKS) as well. But, these are still in early stage trials and so Takeda should benefit from first mover advantage for the meantime. Again, sales estimates are likely conservative as the sleep disorder market is large and faces issues with current treatments being unfavorable for patients.

Peak Sales Estimate: $2-$3 billion

Fazirsiran

With a key partnership with Alylam (ALNY) upcoming rival Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), Takeda is set to enter the oligonucleotide fray with RNAi therapy fazirsiran. This therapy uses the body’s natural mechanisms to degrade mutant proteins in those who are suffering from alpha-1 antitrpsin deficiency, or AATD. There are a few benefits to this therapy. One, the disease exhibits a buildup of misfolded proteins in the liver, leading to disease and fibrosis, and oligonucleotides like the siRNA of this therapy are best administered with an IV in lipid nanoparticles to the liver. Arrowhead, as a close follower to Alnylam, also employs GalNaC technology to coat the nucleotides for increased uptake into cells and reduced breakdown of the RNA in the bloodstream. This should limit adverse effects.

Thanks to another 50/50 profit share, Takeda is set to benefit from this next-generational therapeutic modality. Although, as with Alnylam, there may be plenty of revenue growth but the profits may be more difficult to come by due to the higher cost of manufacturing and developing these complex therapeutics. The small patient population helps as specialty products demand a premium. I find the estimates to be fairly accurate for this therapy, only as Alnylam and peers still barely reach $5 billion in RNA-based therapies after half a decade of approvals combined. The rollout may be slower than expected due to manufacturing needs, so keep that in mind.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion

Mezagitamab

Takeda’s last key late-stage offering potential is anti-CD-38 antibody, mezagitamab, which is a next-generation antibody treatment specifically for the rare autoimmune disease, immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The drug is more targeted and selective for CD38, allowing for some potential to outcompete off-label or expanded indications of Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) rituximab/Rituxan or Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) /Genmab’s (GMAB) daratumumab/Darzalex.

There are a few crucial points to consider for this therapy, alongside the key breakthrough and fast track designations. First, the antibody is highly selective for CD-38, allowing for reduced side effects and higher efficacy in CD-38 mediated diseases, which are primarily related to blood disorders. Second, antibody therapies are fairly mature, but profitable entities, which will allow for stable profit generation by Takeda. By starting as a second line treatment for a rare disease, the company will have limited competition and revenues, but will be able to use profits to expand their indications into larger patient populations. This should be a fairly easy target to meet with Takeda’s current experience.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion

Elritercept

Once again, Takeda has partnered with a biotech company to access global rights to a novel, potentially best-in-class therapeutic. This time, TAK has partnered with Keros Therapeutics (KROS) to develop a drug that promotes blood cell production to treat myelodysplastic syndromes. The current competition is Bristol Myers Squibb’s luspatercept/Reblozyl that earns over $1 billion per year, but may be outcompeted by the efficacy of elritercept that is able to increase both blood cell and platelet production (rather than just blood cells with luspatercept). While it will require a solid commercialization strategy, the opportunity is present to eat away at BMS’s blockbuster market share or grow their own niche. However, we must continue to look at the P3 data to truly weigh the competitive potential. While one of the riskier bets of the bunch, the reward may also exceed expectations for that very reason.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion.

Financial Analysis

So, considering the highest peak sales, we reach $20 billion in revenues. Timing is important too, as these therapies will approach these peaks around the year 2030 to 2031, which is when cash cow Entyvio begins to lose patent protection. Takeda has also faced headwinds from the loss of exclusivity for Shire’s Vyvanse which has led to flat revenues over the past five years when not considering the top-line growth due to the acquisition. This is similar to the period between 2013 and 2019 where Takeda lost exclusivity on several blockbusters, but failed to have a strong pipeline to replace those revenues. Now however, Takeda has a much stronger late stage pipeline that should come into play over the next few years.

Along with weak topline organic growth of sales, Takeda has struggled with margin expansion. While EBITDA margins have remained fairly stable around the low 30s%, net income margins have been quite weak, often negative in some quarters. The poor bottom end performance has been due to the attempted restructuring of internal and Shire operations, along with costs associated with the highly advanced pipeline. Six major candidates in late stage trials is no spare change, but financial conservatism is not the company’s strong point since the firm attempted a global rollout in the 2000s. As part of the restructuring, Takeda has sold off numerous non-core assets, so with time this should improve the revenue mix towards higher margin products, the ones I have discussed in the pipeline especially, so I do expect margin expansion to be possible over the coming years.

Takeda will certainly benefit from increased profit generation from the current assets in development. Most important will be debt reduction, with current leverage above safe levels at 3.2x net debt/EBITDA (TTM). Investors have not been treated well, as valuations have fallen and risk increased over the years. There is one positive: stable cash flow generation. This highlights the ship is righting itself and Takeda’s balance sheet should improve from here. Once debt is reduced, the dividend should continue growing steadily, and shares can be bought back, all leading to a rising valuation.

Outlook

If Takeda is successful in their endeavor to reach $20 billion in revenues from the six discussed therapeutic products by 2030-31, there is no doubt significant returns are possible for investors. None of this success is priced in at a current 1.5x P/S (LTM). However, the math is extremely risky still until these drugs are approved. This is why the valuation remains suppressed as Takeda investors want a show, not tell situation. Let’s break down the opportunity.

Scenario 1: 100% Success

In this scenario, Takeda has all therapies approved and meets the $20 billion revenue target, let's say in 2031. These therapies are both strategically and scientifically sound, allowing for operating income margins to average 25%. Why? Some are small molecules that cost little to manufacture, but will hold leading market positions. Others are antibodies that provide significant aid to patients, deserving a premium. Others are new modalities or rare diseases that also demand premiums due to the innovation and small patient populations that support high prices to allow a return on investment. This means Takeda may earn $5 billion in additional profit annually, rounded down to $4 billion to account for taxes.

However, we must also remove about $1.2 billion in dividend payments. This brings total profit down to $2.8 billion. Added to current therapy earnings of ~$1 billion, that is $3.8 billion. At a P/E of 20, this is a $76 billion market cap, or 10% per year in returns over five years from now. Fiddling around with the numbers, such as removing profit for debt reduction, or increasing the valuation some, generally offset each other so this figure seems to be a fairly manageable target. However, Scenario 2 will show how it is a bit risky to go by management’s expectations.

Scenario 2: 50% Success

The key issues at hand are that not all therapies are guaranteed to be approved. Therapies can also underperform expectations, particularly due to the competitive rivals I discussed for each therapy. Project Alpha from MIT calculates the probability of success for clinical trials, and found that there is a 67.8% chance for a drug to go from Phase 3 to Approval. We must also lower that due to post approval risks such as manufacturing issues, commercialization stalls, and broader competition. Therefore, we can conservatively discount Takeda’s outlook by 50%. One of the biggest reasons why one would favor this scenario is due to Takeda’s weak reorganization after the Shire acquisition. This is an essential fact for why there is no premium attributed to the company at present. $1.9 billion in earnings in five years is in fact a -5% per year return if the valuation falls to 20x P/E.

Scenario 3: 75% Success

The problem with this scenario is that it does not account for the scientific grounds of each therapy. Takeda has chosen well. The pipeline is incredibly efficient and checks off many factors that increase the probability of success. For example, zasocitinib was computationally developed for incredible binding ability to the already de-risked and proven TYK2 inhibition class. So there are already approved molecules, but this drug is potentially safer and more effective due to reduced off-target effects. Also, oveporexton, fazisiran, and rusfertide all leverage the fact that they are first-in-class therapies that fill unmet needs, allowing for some flexibility if the data is not perfect. In rare diseases, the Project Alpha data shows that rare diseases in Blood and Clotting and Immunological indications have over 70% chance of approval from P3. This highlights the ability of scientific strategy to fulfill operational success. Takeda has done well, so the biggest risk remains commercialization.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe a success rate of 75% may be more accurate. This will lead to about $2.85 billion in earnings per year. If the valuation rightly increases significantly, and margins are above my conservative 25% rate for these therapies, then returns can be in the mid double digits from current levels. My Scenario 3 is therefore what I feel more confident about. My strategy will be to consider accumulating shares at current levels and take advantage of continued releases of positive data. If this happens there may be a considerable surge in valuation and share price as the market learns of the opportunity. However, I will be keen to trim on any bull runs as there is downside risk due to Scenario 2.

The sell on good news and buy back at lower valuations ploy will likely be the most conservative and profitable strategy. It’s always best to not get too attached to any biopharma stock, even if you are an income investor enjoying Takeda’s high dividend. Beware the company making the correct choice and not increasing the dividend significantly over time. The total returns will be greater overall and the future will look bright if debt can be reduced and they continue to adeptly generate a high-quality pipeline. Anyway, let me know what you think of this strategy.
